summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that working longer hours is a counterproductive strategy for business success, particularly for startups and small software companies. Drawing on his experience as both a Japanese salaryman working 70-hour weeks and a side-project entrepreneur averaging four hours weekly, he demonstrates that productivity should be measured by business value created, not hours invested. He introduces the concept of a 'pseudo-wage' to evaluate whether tasks are worth doing personally, then presents three concrete techniques: outsourcing low-value work, automating processes, and eliminating unproductive time. His freelancer content strategy generates $1,300/month in sales from five minutes of monthly oversight, proving that leverage beats effort.
key_points: Working longer hours is not a defensible competitive advantage — competitors can always match your hours, and well-resourced companies will always have more total man-hours than you
The 'pseudo-wage' concept lets you assign dollar values to tasks and compare them rationally, revealing that many common activities earn far below your target hourly rate
Outsourcing content creation to freelancers generated $1,300/month in sales from only five minutes of monthly management — results impossible to achieve by simply working more hours
Process improvement compounds over time: automating freelancer workflows went from 60 minutes/month to 5 minutes/month through iterative refinement
Most business blogging value follows a power law — 90% of value comes from fewer than 10% of posts, making long-form essays dramatically more efficient than frequent short posts
money_quotes: The notion that working longer hours is correlated to better business results is a pernicious social pathology.
Take it from a Japanese salaryman, guys: it is a disease so vicious that in addition to hobbling businesses it damages society itself.
Programmers at most companies work on a schedule designed to maximize the productivity of illiterate 18th century water loom operators, so expecting rationality might be excessively optimistic.
If you want to earn $100 an hour, you'd better not busy yourself with $5 an hour tasks.
No middle American schoolmarm perceives additional value over having my saliva on the envelope her software came in.
A startup's most important product is the process the startup uses to create products.
Five minutes isn't zero minutes, either, so I still have work to do.
Working for the sake of work is a waste of time, resources, and human potential.
key_insight: Business productivity should be measured in value created per hour, not hours worked, and the highest-leverage move is replacing low-value labor with outsourcing and automation rather than simply working longer.
